definition: Immigration drove significant labor supply expansion from 2022-2025, with CBO revising net immigration to ~3.3M in 2023 and 3-4M workers above pre-pandemic trend, mechanically raising potential GDP by ~0.2-0.5pp annually via Solow accounting. This is now a decaying tailwind under policy tightening.
evidence: CBO revised net immigration to ~3.3M in 2023
BLS data confirms 3-4M workers above pre-pandemic trend
Solow accounting is mechanical: more workers at average productivity = more output
pair_0 judged this decomposition as supported
CBO estimate of ~3.3M net immigration in 2023
Vacancy decline from ~12M to ~8M without unemployment rising
Solow accounting: 3-4M workers x ~$80k/worker ≈ 0.3-0.4pp GDP growth
Concentration in lower-skill service occupations (hospitality, food services, construction, healthcare) relieved sectors with most acute post-COVID wage pressure
Most convergent factual claim across all 8 agents
open_questions: Is immigration being double-counted — can it raise potential GDP AND keep labor market loose simultaneously?
How quickly will policy tightening reverse the labor supply gains?
Does extensive growth (more workers) vs intensive growth (higher productivity per worker) justify equity multiples?
What fraction of the vacancy decline is attributable to immigration vs. other factors?
How much of the disinflationary effect was temporary vs. structural?
